replies.author: @Doomweapon66
replies.text:
@ariaroflcakes "'New Bungie' listening to players?" Give me a break. I can't tell you how many times over the time I played Destiny (which goes from the D1 Beta to the end of Final Shape) where I've seen people ask for multiple features, tweaks to specific balancing stuff (i.e. "new Bungie" maybe laying off on the excessive nerfs they spam as is the infamous case with Dead Man's Tale having infamously switched between 2 shotting people with minimal effort in PvP or you getting destroyed for even trying to use it), and so on and "new Bungie" consistently ignored it.
Hell, I haven't played D2 in a while but my understanding last time I tangentially checked on it via Aztecross or one of my friends telling me of it was it was only conveniently after Final Shape with stuff like the Star Wars collab where a lot of the features me and everyone else I knew were hoping to be added for years finally get into the game when all of us collectively gave up on it and gave up hope that it'd ever be run in an intelligent manner.
Mind you, I should also note what I've said thus far has been ignoring features and changes "new Bungie" added that literally nobody asked for. My understanding is there's effectively a system now equivalent to a "star rating" system akin to something like Hoyo game weapons and all the weapons I used to have would be "1 star variants" and you need "3 star variants" to get the best damage out of a given weapon. And you have to farm for those. Which also therefore means I would have to regrind my entire arsenal over again just to get those "3 star variants" of my existing guns just so I can squeeze out enough damage.

Unfortunately, I would say the vast majority of people who are not "in the know" via playing things like Destiny for as long as I had (and I would argue I gave "new Bungie" way too many "second chances" by even going to the end of Final Shape) have a very bad issue of conflating "new Bungie" (post-D2 Forsaken Bungie because Forsaken was when the last major "old head" from the Halo days, Marty O' Donnell, got forced out) and the old Halo-era Bungie as being "one and the same." 
They are *not* the same entity in anything except for name. The vast majority of people at "new Bungie" never even touched Bungie Halo code, much less OG Marathon's code. These people don't have the same "vision" as the OG Bungie guys and I would argue the vast majority aren't even fit to spit shine the shoes of people like Jason Jones, Marcus Lehto, Jaime Griesemer, Marty O' Donnell, etc. 

And I've seen how the "new Bungie" devs handle stuff like their game balancing. They *always* have sperged the f out whenever a new "meta" ever popped up in D2 and they immediately would nerf that stuff within weeks at the most after the strats pop up and become widely adopted (i.e. they nuked the Golden Gun sniper very shortly after Final Shape raid released because people were using it with Celestial Nighthawk for really high damage totals, they nuked Vex Mythoclast when "new Bungie" rather predictably caused it to do the exact same sort of stuff it did to PvP in D1, they've been warring with Dead Man's Tale since it's release because it's always had a history of 2 headshot hipfires or 3-4 shots and you cease to exist because it fires too slow for 3 shots to be viable).

I've seen "new Marathon's" gunplay. It literally is just D2 gunplay but reskinned. I'll throw shade as Smash JT from the clip I saw of him trying to tap fire an enemy with what looked to be a 900 RPM SMG but I'll also point out that's a problem in D2 as well. D2's weapons basically require you to use them as their intended ranges and playstyle if it's not an exotic and I will both throw shade at that (because some weapon types just suck in D2 straight up) and give props to "new Bungie" for remaining fairly consistent on that.
Here's where I find "new Marathon's" gunplay being reskinned D2 gunplay to be a problem though: There appears to be no such thing as exotics or uniques (i.e. Mountaintop type of grenade launchers, etc.) in "new Marathon's" weapons sandbox. This means your options are much more limited. And those limited options will immediately show their strengths and weakness and people will resultingly figure out much faster what weapon types straight up suck in most scenarios versus the ones that are good. And if my guess that these weapons are probably a "original D2 launch" type of set, preconfigured weapons situation, then that'll only cement the "meta" even harder. And "new Bungie" historically with D2 always *hated* the "meta" even becoming a thing. 
So my guess is fairly predictable based on that knowledge: 
"New Marathon" has a "meta" set up within days after release because people very quickly figure out which weapons are universally solid, which ones suck, which ones are good but niche, etc. 
People, in their attempts to remain competitive (because, you know, this is "new Bungie." We "can't" have old style Bungie Halo PvP where competitive play came second and casual "party game-esque" PvP was the priority), will consolidate their weapon loadouts to the "meta" just to deal with less headache. 
"New Bungie" sees the "meta" becoming a thing and nerfs the top performing weapons because they're "too good" (something I learned the hard way with D2 as well is sometimes the "meta" exists in the way it does because other options sometimes just flat out suck. And I also had to learn the hard way that "new Bungie" doesn't exactly bother with buffing up under-performers sometimes and sometimes if they do, the buffs are more of a "look, we buffed it" rather than an actual meaningful buff).
The result of the nerf is people find the next best weapons to use as the "default choices," the cycle repeats, and "new Bungie" still doesn't learn the lesson that they've had to deal with since D1 even. That nerfs sometimes don't solve the problem because it's not necessarily a "this gun is OP issue" and it's a "literally every other gun sucks D" issue.

Here's your reality. All that Marathon has done at this point is proved that it might not be completely DOA on release, and I say might because we still have to see how the weekend plays out.

Many people that support Marathon feel the need to bash Arc Raiders to try and prop up Marathon and this is a problem, because Arc Raiders is showing twice the player counts of marathon at this point even with Marathon being the big release of the weekend. meaning that there is a real possibility it's player count is not going to grow much.  Attacking more popular games is never a winning strategy for player bases, it often leads to the decline and possible failure of the side doing it.

Finally even if Marathon avoids the Concord status of being DOA on it's release, that does not make it successful. It still needs to hit certain higher thresh-holds hopefully against lower costs to actually not still be a failure.  If it doesn't make a profit it may linger on for some time but not actually succeed, even if it doesn't crash and burn out of the gate.

These are the realities we are looking at right now. If it was passing more of these realities I'd say so.  I've done so in the past for other games.

personally I have other issues that I've seen with the game design that I hve to look deeper into.  But I don't use them to call the game a failure or success.

replies.author: @Quandry1
@Doomweapon66
replies.text:
God forbid you have something like epilepsy and you set your mouse sensitivity too high and try to turn too fast either. There's a reason why I will consistently argue that if I were in charge of "new Marathon" at this point, I would've dragged the entire art department kicking and screaming into a conference room, strapped them to the chairs, put up a slideshow presentation using the color coded ranking system of the elites from the Bungie Halo games (more specifically Halo 1 and 2 for even better demonstration of my points), and would've forced all of them to sit through a class about color design, enemy design, environment design, and how all 3 interact with each other.
Very rough TL:DR: of that is Halo 1 and 2 elites have extremely distinct color coding for their ranks and said ranks use bright saturated versions of those colors (going from lowest to highest ranks, it's blue, red, white, brown/pale silver for stealth elites, gold, and black for spec ops elites. Spec ops elites in Halo 2 are notable for also having a very dark blackish purple color scheme alongside the pure black color scheme). The end result is in the vast majority of situations, you can look at an elite at range without a sniper scope or anything and immediately be able to tell what elite rank you're looking at and be able to prioritize targets based on everything else around him. This exact same rule applies with jackals and grunts too. 
And none of the Covenant enemies ever are "impossible" to see against the environmental backdrops either. Delta Halo in Halo 2 is a great example of this where at the end of the level just before the very first Honor Guards you fight in the entire game are at, you can see on the outskirts of the structure some elites patrolling roughly around ranges where you would use a sniper if you wanted to take them out. The structure itself is an ancient ruins tanned brown type of color but all the elites on Normal or Easy are either red or blue rank elites. This means you can visually see them and immediately know what you're looking at even from far ranges. 
It's not something you actively think about too much when you play but I would argue it's genius color design around the enemies because you can almost immediately tell what you're looking at from even really far distances and be able to gather info on the fight you're about to get into just from a glance.

author: @MistaOppritunity
text: I remember watching the trailer for Marathon. I may have been in the minority, but I honestly found the character design visually appealing, and the premise for the game interesting, and I had immediate interest in it. Then it was revealed to be another multiplayer extraction shooter, and I went, well this game isn't for me. But I was still curious because of the discourse surrounding the game, so I asked my friend who plays extraction shooters. His immediate thoughts were, "it's just another extraction shooter." I'm not saying that I don't think the game could survive or even thrive. People could be entering cautiously because of the controversy surrounding it, and player counts are going to peak higher. But I will say that in a market oversaturated with games in the same genre as it, they have their work cut out for them. You have to try harder to succeed when people are tired of your product. Not only because they are sick of it, but you are actively competing with every product that came before yours and may still actively be played. I won't hold my breath for Marathon to be a good game, but I'll be pleasantly surprised to see that Bungie put out a good game if it survives.

My problem with Marathon is that it is so artistically, tonally, and mechanically detached from the Marathon games that I grew up playing that it is completely unrecognizable.
Imagine taking the exact same game and calling it "Doom".  *That's* how completely detached from the original games this is.  Look up the original art and gameplay of the Marathon Trilogy, or hell, play it yourself via the AlephOne project, then tell me this game calling itself "Marathon" is in any way related.  The art direction alone turns me off this game more than any gameplay issues ever could.
